title: 30 The Metal Gear Solid 2 of NME
display_title: 30 The Metal Gear Solid 2 of NME
wikitext:
[]: 3.0: The Metal Gear Solid 2 of NME?             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 9:28 pm I've brought this up several times, but I feel that I might as well make a topic for it so I can word my thoughts better. As many of you guys are undoubtably aware, 3.0 is quite a divisive film. Not only does it go against the viewer's expectations of what they were hoping for after 2.0, it also makes the characters more or less pawns to some greater plan, with several key plot points mirroring those seen in the original series and even some of the first two films, but in a rather twisted manner. I admit that I have spoken out on many of the choices made for it, as I felt that the execution was quite off. However, all of this reminds me of one of my favorite video games of all time; Metal Gear Solid 2 Sons Of Liberty. That game also left many players divided over the direction that the story took, with many feeling betrayed and used by the writers. As more people began to analyze it however, the game slowly but surely began to be seen as one of the gutsiest moves ever pulled in the medium. To summarize, Metal Gear Solid 2 was the sequel to the first Metal Gear Solid, considered by many to be one of the greatest games ever made at the time, and certainly one of the most popular games for the original Playstation. So when the sequel was announced, it inevitably became the most hyped game of 2001. Early demos and preview videos promised another epic adventure where the player controlled the series protagonist Solid Snake, this time infiltrating a gigantic tanker, to stop the production of yet another Metal Gear. When the game was finally released however, the game instead revealed that it was focused on a whole different character, Raiden, who is essentially the complete opposite of Snake, and that's not even getting into the mind screw of a plot, coupled with a rather depressing ending, which ended with the villains winning. Unsurprisingly, fans rioted. How does this relate to 3.0 exactly? Well it is mainly due to the themes and methods which the designers used to fool the players and toy with their expectations. If you want, check out this link, which explains it far better than I can hope to. [url]http://www.deltaheadtranslation.com/MGS2/DOTM_TOC.htm[/url] To save some time however, let me try to quickly summarize. MGS2 was meant as a post-modern deconstruction on the nature of sequels and playing games as power fantasies. Many had enjoyed the first because they got to play as someone who they considered to be a complete badass, and wanted to be just like him, despite the fact that Snake was meant as a realistic portrayal of what action-heroes are like mentally. As a result, Raiden was created as a sort of wake up call to just how hellish becoming a badass is, and how easily manipulated one can be in their desire to be a hero. In addition, the game took several shots at sequels in general, or at least those with very similar premises as the first. The entirety of the game was set on a gigantic oil rig, later revealed to be a Metal Gear, called the Big Shell. Its locations and scenarios more or less mirrored those seen in the first game, only this time, each one was twisted in some way to be a far less original and thrilling experience. Even the characters took on several of the roles seen in the original. All of this was designed by the villains to see if they could control someone in an extreme situation. Not content with just that though, the game ultimately took shots at the series itself, and to a greater extent, the themes and story of the first game. MGS1 concluded with Snake deciding to settle down with the love interest, Meryl Silverburgh, finally deciding to not let his genes of being an enhanced super-soldier define his character, which ties into the game's message, to choose life, and then live. The second game completely undid this whole development, beginning with Snake once again acting like the controlled soldier he was, with his attempt at being normal ending in failure. Supporting this was also the character of Raiden, who throughout the course of the game, was always held on a leash by the villains, and every action of his pre-determined by them. So with all that that I've said, what was the point of all this? Apparently, the creator, Hideo Kojima, didn't really want to make a sequel to MGS1, but fan demand required that he create one. So with MGS2, he tried to make a game that, by playing with their expectations and essentially undoing all the progress the first game had made, would slap the fans in the face with reality, and force them to question if it was all worth it. Now how does this all relate to 3.0? Well think about it. 3.0 was a very highly anticipated film to what many consider to be one of the best anime films of all time, fooled the fanbase with its previews, and when released, swiftly divided it. Not to mention that, like MGS2, it essentially undid most of the progress made in the first two films, had the main character be little more than a pawn of the main villains, acted as a rebuttal to those who wanted the hero to be a badass, and concluded on a confusing and depressing note. Sound familiar? Now what exactly was Anno, like Kojima, trying to say here? Did he too believe that the viewers had missed the point of the first two films, so he tried to slap them in the face with reality as hard as he could? Was he simply tired of telling the same story over again? Whatever the case, it is interesting to see the parallels. Back to MGS2 and Kojima, the fan backlash caused him to explain and clear up everything seven years later with Metal Gear Solid 4 Guns Of The Patriots. Will Anno also do something similar, considering just how fractured the fanbase is, not to mention all the lingering plot threads, holes, and unanswered questions that 3.0 has raised? What do you guys think?

[]:             Quote Post by monitoradiation » Wed Oct 23, 2013 10:01 pm I haven't played MGS because I dislike Kojima and his ridiculous antics. Plus I watch movies, not play them. Regarding your overall take on the "sequels being made to troll/deconstruct the artform", I rather take it as somewhat of a jerk move. Playing around with people's expectations generally isn't a nice thing to do. Especially because there's no consent or agreement beforehand. Having fun with your own project is one thing - but to have fun at other people's expense when they paid for a product after seeing an advertisement of some kind is low. I understand that sometimes sequel take another route from the previous. Sure. But to construct a ruse and then deliver something completely differently is asinine. This is like if I paid for a steak but got a bowl of what looks like soup instead. Then when I ask the waiter, the waiter insists that it's steak. It might be very fragrant. It might be very nice. I might enjoy it more than I would have if it were a piece of meat. But my trust for the restaurant to continue to keep my business will be vastly diminished. So my message to those two (Anno and Kojima) would be: If you want to troll the audience's expectations, dont troll them with what you've shown them. Let them generate their own expectations, and break those instead. Don't make promises that you don't intend to keep; because regardless of how "artistically" you break them, you're still a jerk.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Wed Oct 23, 2013 10:27 pm Anyone who is surprised that Hideaki Anno favors dramatic left turns, outside-the-box storytelling and unexpected plot twists really hasn't been paying attention. Since day one, Evangelion has been about questioning the solid ground you thought stood beneath your feet. Anno's never been too invested in "keeping promises", and thank god for that.

[]:             Quote Post by Gendo'sPapa » Wed Oct 23, 2013 10:42 pm Don't see why this is the case? Maybe this analogy would make sense if for the first ten minutes Shinji was the protagonist then the film switched over to Kaworu for the remainder of the film with Shinji showing up in the last 2 minutes to do something. Either way, Anno is doing what he does. Which is why he is still among my five favorite filmmakers in the world working today! "Hideaki Anno doesn't do what Hideaki Anno does for Hideaki Anno. Hideaki Anno does what Hideaki Anno does because Hideaki Anno is... Hideaki Anno." - quote by James Cameron

[]:             Quote Post by monitoradiation » Wed Oct 23, 2013 10:44 pm Warren Peace wrote: Anyone who is surprised that Hideaki Anno favors dramatic left turns, outside-the-box storytelling and unexpected plot twists really hasn't been paying attention. Since day one, Evangelion has been about questioning the solid ground you thought stood beneath your feet. Anno's never been too invested in "keeping promises", and thank god for that.

[]:             Quote Post by WunderBah » Wed Oct 23, 2013 11:02 pm Gendo'sPapa wrote: Don't see why this is the case? Maybe this analogy would make sense if for the first ten minutes Shinji was the protagonist then the film switched over to Kaworu for the remainder of the film with Shinji showing up in the last 2 minutes to do something. Either way, Anno is doing what he does. Which is why he is still among my five favorite filmmakers in the world working today! "Hideaki Anno doesn't do what Hideaki Anno does for Hideaki Anno. Hideaki Anno does what Hideaki Anno does because Hideaki Anno is... Hideaki Anno." - quote by James Cameron

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Wed Oct 23, 2013 11:07 pm monitoradiation wrote: I'm saying that the expectation artists are allowed to break that fit under "artistic license" are the ones that the audience makes for themselves. If someone creates expectations explicitly either via statements or advertisement and breaks THOSE, it loses the subtlety altogether and becomes kind of kitsch/lame. Notice how I never said this directly applied to Anno because the credit scenes from 2.22 can still fit within the larger narrative of the timeskip. My message was offered as a caution.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Wed Oct 23, 2013 11:24 pm monitoradiation wrote: That's kind of missing the point. I'm saying that the expectation artists are allowed to break that fit under "artistic license" are the ones that the audience makes for themselves. If someone creates expectations explicitly either via statements or advertisement and breaks THOSE, it loses the subtlety altogether and becomes kind of kitsch/lame. Notice how I never said this directly applied to Anno because the credit scenes from 2.22 can still fit within the larger narrative of the timeskip. My message was offered as a caution.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Wed Oct 23, 2013 11:27 pm LightDragonman wrote: What do you guys think?

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Thu Oct 24, 2013 12:28 am Don't see why this is the case? Maybe this analogy would make sense if for the first ten minutes Shinji was the protagonist then the film switched over to Kaworu for the remainder of the film with Shinji showing up in the last 2 minutes to do something.

[]:             Quote Post by monitoradiation » Thu Oct 24, 2013 8:37 am Warren Peace wrote: So you're pissed that he didn't "film" 2.0's preview, basically? Well, I can't understand being all that broken up about it, but okay. Still, I hardly think think that represents some kind of unbreakable contract with the audience or something. This is like asking, "why didn't George Lucas tease Darth Vader's real identity earlier? He told us he was the guy who killed Luke's dad!"

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Thu Oct 24, 2013 1:46 pm monitoradiation wrote: ... What? I explicitly stated that 2.22's credit scene was fine... Nor am i "pissed" at anything Anno did. In fact, can you just re-read what I wrote? I'm not sure if I can do better than I already wrote.
